🔐 LABEL (Secret):
scott.jacobucci@elpaso.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
[REDACTED],

The “other” course you’re asking about is the Cypresswood Classic (sometimes just called “the Member course”).  
Tradition is the newer, tournament-style layout—more forced carries, bigger greens complexes, and the conditioning is usually a notch above.  Classic is shorter, tree-lined, a little more forgiving off the tee, but the greens are smaller and you’ve got to keep it below the hole.  If you’re a 10-handicap or better you’ll probably find Tradition worth the extra $17; if you just want a quick, fun round the Classic is fine.  Either way, book through the city site—both courses share the same starter shack so you can switch when you get there if the tee sheet allows.

Address for GPS: 4343 Cypresswood Dr., Spring, TX 77388.  From 45N take the Cypresswood/Research Forest exit, head west two miles, course is on the right just past the high-school stadium.  Range opens at 6:30, first tee time 7:10.

See you out there—bring the 60° if you play Tradition, pin locations were brutal yesterday.

Safe travels,

[REDACTED]

You are literally down—horizontal, cheek to the chilled linoleum of a corridor that smells of iodine and burnt coffee. Subject: Re: Updated Settlement Location Codes for PG&E – Effective Immediately

All,

To avoid any confusion in tomorrow’s day-ahead run, the CAISO Master File has already been refreshed with the new keys.  
If you are pulling XML feeds, the element <SettlementLocation> will now return:

   <SettlementLocation code="PCGB">PG&E Bay Area</SettlementLocation>
   <SettlementLocation code="PGAB">PG&E Area B</SettlementLocation>
   <SettlementLocation code="COTB">PG&E Cottonwood</SettlementLocation>

The old codes (PCG1, PGAE, COTP) will return NULL as of 00:01 today.  
If your scheduling software caches these values, force a refresh before 10:00 a.m. or you will receive a “Location Not Found” error on submissions.

Settlement statements for 04/29 will still reference the old codes; statements for 04/30 forward will use the new ones.  
No retro-active restatement is planned.

Questions to me or Kourtney.

Carolyn

      ,Copyright 2000 EEI. All rights reserved.

EEI's [REDACTED] is proud to present an [REDACTED] 
providing an overview of the [REDACTED]'s 
(NERC) recently released [REDACTED] 2000-2009, the reliability of 
bulk electric systems in [REDACTED].

The reliability of the electric generation and transmission systems serving 
[REDACTED] has come under increased federal government, state regulator, 
and customer scrutiny. Generation capacity margins have decreased and the 
bulk electric systems are subjected to flows in magnitudes and directions not 
contemplated when they were designed or for which there is minimal operating 
experience.

Each year, the [REDACTED] (RAS) of the North 
[REDACTED] (NERC) reviews the overall reliability 
of existing and planned electric generation and transmission systems in North 
America. This year's [REDACTED] 2000-2009 report presents:

*An assessment of electric generation and transmission reliability through 
2009

*An assessment of the generation resource adequacy of each Interconnection in 
[REDACTED]

*A discussion of key issues affecting reliability of future electric supply

*Regional assessments of electric supply reliability, including issues of 
specific Regional concern

Presenting an overview of this year's [REDACTED] is [REDACTED], Jr., Chairman, [REDACTED], and General 
Manager, Ventures, [REDACTED].

Our guest moderator is [REDACTED], [REDACTED], NERC

To provide questions and commentary we have [REDACTED], [REDACTED] of 
Transmission, Dynegy

A copy of the [REDACTED] 2000-2009 is available from the NERC web 
site at http://www.nerc.com/~pc/syscond.html.

November 21, 2000 1 p.m. ET

to take about one hour. [REDACTED] at the conclusion of the E-Forum could take up to

E-Forums are real-time, interactive presentations, created, produced and 
distributed by EEI. The visual presentation is delivered over the Internet, 
while the audio portion is delivered through a conference phone call. You can 
participate in an E-Forum using any computer with Internet access and a 
telephone.

To fully participate in the E-Forum, you will need:

- Access to the Internet and a web browser.
- A separate voice phone line and a phone to dial into the audio conference 
call. Cell phones can be used.
- Upon confirmation of your registration, you will be given complete 
instructions including the URL (with Password/ID) and a the conference phone 
number.
- Slides used in the presentation will also be e-mailed to you prior to the 
forum and will soon be available over the Internet.

For more information on EEI's E-Forums, contact [REDACTED], Director, State 
Competitive and [REDACTED] at 202-508-5523 moldak@eei.org or [REDACTED], Coordinator at (202) 508-5522

distributed by EEI. The visual presentation is delivered over the Internet, 
while the audio portion is delivered through a conference phone call. You can 
participate in an E-Forum using any computer with Internet access and a 
telephone.

number.

Subject: Re: Q3 Reporting Adjustments – Urgent Review Needed

Connie,

catfish.

and five guys stand up.

5. A member of the church requests to be buried in his 4-wheel-drive truck
because, "It ain't never been in a hole it couldn't get out of." 
6. When it rains, everyone is smiling. 
7. Prayers regarding the weather are standard practice.

9. The pastor wears boots.

Team,

The California Power Exchange just faxed over a formal Event-of-Default notice (copy attached).  
Effective close of business today they are suspending Sempra Energy Trading’s trading privileges, pulling our $350 million unsecured credit line, and demanding immediate posting of $287 million in cash collateral for outstanding mark-to-market exposure on the book.

Key points:

1. Collateral call is based on yesterday’s CAISO spark-spread curve; our stress-test yesterday showed only $112 mm exposure – they have added a 35 % systematic haircut “to reflect heightened systemic risk.”

3. Their notice cites “failure to remedy a Material Adverse Change” triggered by yesterday’s downgrade of Sempra parent to BBB- by S&P.  Moody’s put us on negative watch this morning – rumor is they move tonight.

The program this year has been designed to confront the uncertainties that are reshaping our industry—geopolitical volatility, the accelerating energy transition, and the evolving expectations of investors, policymakers, and society at large. In addition to the plenary sessions, we have expanded the number of executive round-tables and private workshops so that participants can drill down on the issues most relevant to their organizations.  

Among the confirmed speakers are the ministers of energy from Saudi Arabia, Norway, and Nigeria; the CEOs of [REDACTED], [REDACTED], and [REDACTED]; and the governors of California and Alberta. As in past years, Chatham House Rule will apply to the off-record sessions, ensuring candor and the free exchange of views.  

If you register before 15 January we will secure the Hyatt Regency Houston at the preferential CERAWeek rate and include complimentary ground transportation to and from all venues. After that date rooms will be subject to availability at prevailing prices.  

I do hope you will make time in your schedule to be with us. CERAWeek has always derived its value from the quality of the people in the room, and

All –

**Executive Summary (abridged for distribution)**

1. **Temperature Outlook**  
   a. Analog winters (’77-’78, ’85-’86, ’96-’97) argue for a –1.4 °C deviation below the 30-yr normal across the upper-Mid-Atlantic and New England.  
   b. Confidence is highest (≈70 %) for a persistent –NAO ridge/trough couplet during Jan-Feb, implying 12–16 more HDDs than last year for Con-Ed and 9–11 for NGrid.  

2. **Precipitation & Snowfall**  
   a. Weak El Niño Modoki signature (+0.9 °C Niño-3.4) favors a mid-tropospheric moisture streamer into the Ohio Valley; snow-water-equivalent 105–115 % of normal for the Marcellus production counties (Tioga, Susquehanna, Bradford).

        Erin, 
        Thanks for the advance copy of the paper.  While there are a number of very good points made in the paper, [REDACTED] has a fundamental problem with EPSA endorsing a new market power test that is more stringent than we think is necessary.  We would recommend that EPSA support the [REDACTED], or perhaps simply take the position in option 5 (no test for bid-based markets operated by an ISO or RTO).  We simply believe that EPSA should not be "negotiating with itself" at this juncture.  That is, we should not assume that the majority of the Commissioners are with [REDACTED].  We should use this paper to argue to the other Commissioners and staff that if they move off Hub and Spoke, they only need to move incrementally, not dramatically.  In making this recommendation we recognize that this may not be enough for FERC and we may not like what we end up with.  But [REDACTED] is simply not prepared to support a position we really don't agree with in the hope that we can avoid an even worse result.  I particularly recall [REDACTED] reservations about these issues (voiced at the meeting a month ago) and am hopeful that we can influence Wood and Brownell in this regard.

        The language that reiterates EPSA's position that FERC's primary focus should be on getting market design right is very well done.  Also the arguments on why it would be disastrous to condition our market based rate authority is well done and timely.

        We recognize that EPSA has been working on this issue for weeks now, but this is not a simple issue and we have needed this time to think it through.  We appreciate your efforts and believe this is an important issue for EPSA.   Thanks, [REDACTED]

…or e-mail her at [REDACTED]@edison.com no later than 5:00 p.m. (EST) Thursday, November 1st so we can incorporate your input before the Friday discussion.

Key issues for your advance review:

1.  Whether EPSA should support the “residual supply” test outlined on pp. 7-9 of the draft, or revert to the narrower “deliverability” screen proposed by staff in Alternative C.  Our wholesale members are split; the Midwest and Northeast groups believe the residual approach gives them more latitude, while the Southeast companies fear it invites FERC to second-guess native-load protections.

2.  The sunset clause (§ III.B).  Staff’s draft would require every holder of MBR authority to re-justify its market power status every five years.  Some of us think a rolling 10-year horizon is more realistic given the lag in constructing new transmission.

3.  The data-request template (Attachment 2).  We have inserted placeholders for the three new spreadsheets FERC is likely to demand.  Please flag any proprietary concerns; we will need a clean “public” version and a redline version before filing.

- Firewall rules older than 90 days that have not been re-certified.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Another example of a regular issues e-mail push I receive...this one from a law-firm.

-----[REDACTED]-----
From: Zimmer, [REDACTED] J [mailto:[REDACTED].J.Zimmer@BAKERNET.com]
Sent: Wednesday, October 17, 2001 3:08 PM
Subject: E-Notes: Treasury and [REDACTED] to [REDACTED] of
[REDACTED]


>  <<...OLE_Obj...>> 
> E-Notes provides regular briefings on new developments in global energy
> and public utility law.
> 
> 
> October 17, 2001
> 
> [REDACTED]
> INTERCONNECTIONS 
> 
> 
> 	[REDACTED]S. Department of Treasury and the IRS are likely to issue
> within the next several months definitive guidance amending the rules
> governing the federal income tax treatment of transfers of, or payments
> for, electricity interties.  The new rules will almost certainly allow
> independent power producers to more freely transfer or pay for an intertie
> on a tax-free basis.  This certainty could remove the necessity to manage
> this issue in negotiations of new interconnection agreements for
> generation facilities.
> 
> The key elements of the current rules likely to be modified include the
> need for a long-term power purchase agreement to avoid tax and
> restrictions on the ability to freely wheel power from the generating
> facility.  The new rules are not likely to directly apply to transfers of,
> or payments for, gas interties, but may, nevertheless, affect the
> circumstances in which gas intertie transfers are tax-free where such
> transfers can be analogized to electric interconnections.
> 
> Federal income tax law has long provided an exception to gross income for
> corporations that receive shareholder and nonshareholder contributions of
> money or other property to capital.  Congress limited that exception in
> 1986, excluding so-called "contributions in aid of construction," or
> "CIACs."  Congress did not define CIACs in the statute, but the
> legislative history indicates that Congress understands CIACs as transfers
> designed to induce or encourage the provision of services to or for the
> benefit of the person making the contribution.
> 
> In 1988, the IRS provided a safe harbor for qualified facilities (QF's)
> within the meaning of the [REDACTED], as amended by the Public
> [REDACTED].  The safe harbor is limited, however, to
> cases where, among other things, the QF has a [REDACTED]
> (PPA) of at least ten (10) years in place and where a dual-use intertie is
> used to transmit only nominal amounts (5% or less) of power back to the
> QF.  Although understandable in 1988, the long-term PPA requirement seems
> misplaced in today's merchant operation environment.  Other aspects of the
> safe harbor, such as the QF requirement, are also dated.  IRS private
> letter rulings over the last decade have not added much clarity or
> certainty.  Uncertainties regarding the federal income tax treatment of
> interties have thus increasingly plagued the electric power and natural
> gas industries and have escalated in negotiations of new interconnection
> agreements.
> 
> 	The new thinking at Treasury and the IRS may have been foreshadowed
> by a recent private letter ruling released by the IRS in late summer, PLR
> 280134021 (dated May 30, 2001, and released August 24, 2001).  The ruling
> concludes that the transfer of an intertie by a QF owner is not gross
> income to a transmission company even though only an undisclosed
> percentage of the output is subject to a 10-year PPA.  The remaining
> output will be sold on the open market through an energy trading company.
> This result is similar to an earlier ruling, PLR 9327019 (April 6, 1993),
> but the new ruling appears more expansive.
> 
> The new guidance is likely to expand the existing safe harbor in one or
> more ways, including loosening or eliminating the PPA requirement and
> relaxation of the QF requirement in favor of increased reliance on an
> updated and expanded de minimus back flow or multi-use test.  
> 
> Baker & McKenzie tax and major projects lawyers are taking an active role
> in providing industry input to the IRS and Treasury, and reflecting these
> developments in the contracting, negotiation and financing of new
> generator and fuel facilities nationwide.
> 
> 
> [REDACTED]
> [REDACTED]
> 
> 
> 
> __________________________________________________________________________
> __
> E-Notes is a publication of Baker & McKenzie.  It does not constitute
> legal advice or a legal opinion on any specific facts or circumstances.
> The contents are intended as general information only.  You are urged to
> consult your attorney concerning your situation and specific legal
> questions you may have.  For further information on the subjects discussed
> in E-Notes, contact [REDACTED],
> mailto:

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
michael.j.zimmer@bakernet.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
[CONTINUATION]

> **PRACTICAL TAKE-AWAYS FOR DEVELOPERS AND LENDERS**
>
> 1. **Delay Nothing.**  The mere prospect of new rules is not a license to postpone negotiations.  Until Treasury issues regulations or a revenue procedure, the 1988 safe-harbor remains the only objective shield.  Counsel should continue to draft “tax-gross-up” clauses that survive only until the earlier of (i) a favorable ruling on the specific facts or (ii) publication of the promised guidance.  
>
> 2. **Document the “Business Purpose.”**  Regardless of how relaxed the PPA requirement becomes, the contribution must still be shown to be “to capital” and not merely a disguised prepayment for service.  A short recital in the interconnection agreement that the intertie is dedicated to the transmission system and will be subject to joint-use or open-access tariffs can help immunize the transaction.  
>
> 3. **QF Status Still Matters—For Now.**  The IRS continues to cite the QF definition in every private ruling.  If your project cannot qualify (for example, because it exceeds the 80-MW cogeneration size limit), consider inserting a special-purpose

SAMPLE #207

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
[REDACTED] this purely political or is the FERC giving the states "real" authority?

Thanks - Jeff
---------------------- Forwarded by [REDACTED]EES on 10/29/2001 07:43 AM ---------------------------


"[REDACTED]" <

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
dsg47@earthlink.net

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
“[REDACTED]” <[REDACTED]@ferc.gov>  
10/29/2001 08:17 AM  

Jeff—

It’s both.  

Yes, the Commission’s new rule (Order 2001-A) hands the states a bigger seat at the table than they’ve had since ‘78, but only inside a fence FERC itself is still building. Think of it as a gated subdivision: the states can pick the paint color, even add a sun-room, yet the footprint, setbacks, and height limits are pre-approved in D.C.  

What’s “real” is the delegation of certification authority on <REDACTED>—states can now block a project on <REDACTED> grounds if they complete the record within 180 days and meet the Federal filing specs. That clock, plus the new interconnection queue rules, gives them leverage they didn’t have before.  

What’s cosmetic is the right to set rates for <REDACTED> transmission; FERC retains final say on any rate that crosses a Regional boundary or exceeds the “just and reasonable” screen. In other words, they can say “no” but can’t unilaterally say “how much.”

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
DJ Smith <dsmith@hmot.com>  
Subject: Draft 1-X – Long-Term Forward Contracts – Comments Due 1/22

All,

Attached is the cleanest version we’ve been able to circulate without triggering the “energy-only” flags in Chairman Tauzin’s office.  Key points that survived the weekend red-line session:

1.  Tenor:  the 15-year cap is gone.  The contracts can now run “through the useful life of the asset” so long as the underlying facility is < 20 yrs old on the contract start date.  (This keeps the nukes and 1970s coal fleets from gaming the strip.)
2.  Credit:  the old “investment-grade only” language was replaced by a two-tier collateral schedule—physically-settled deals get a 5 % MTM threshold, financially-settled 15 %.  Bilateral posting is allowed, but the CFTC gets real-time disclosure on any collateral call > $25 MM.
3.  Index:  we struck the mandatory exchange-trading clause.  Instead, the deal has to reference at least three “widely published” price points.  Staff counsel swears this lets us keep the OTC desks alive without anger

Subject: Re: Cities’ Position on AEP / SWEPCO Settlement Offer – Docket No. 24468

All,

After conferring with the steering-committee members, the Cities are unable to accept the settlement as presently structured.  Our principal objections are set out below.  We will, however, remain available to negotiate through the weekend if AEP/SWEPCO is prepared to move materially on these points.

1.  Fuel-factor true-up  
   •  The Cities cannot support any mechanism that places 100 % of the Texas jurisdiction’s post-test-year fuel-related risk on ratepayers.  We could live with a 50/50 sharing band for variances beyond ±2 %, provided the company files a full-cost, fuel-clause audit every twelve months.  
   •  Any deferred fuel amounts must be trued-up within twenty-four months, not the thirty-six months proposed.

2.  Storm-cost recovery  
   •  We will not agree to securitize the 1999–2000 storm costs.  If the company wishes

This should be done by FERC quickly.  There should be no further delay.  Regardless of the model, FERC should encourage the collaborative process.  She thinks that this should be done by the parties without FERC intervention except to the extent to respond to FERC orders. (I think FERC staff should be involved or it will get bogged down).  Parties should reconvene within 15 days after FERC's order adopting a model and the FERC order should direct the participants (like us) to submit a joint proposal within 45 days thereafter.  She recommends to FERC that parties be allowed to file comments to FERC within 20 days (however, she recommends against permitting reply comments since they may prove counterproductive to the collaborative nature of the mediation process.)  [REDACTED] will then issue an order that can adopt her recommendations, may reject her recommendations, or change something.  I would expect that the Commission would adopt many of her recommendations, while allowing further mediated-type discussions on some issues (hopefully, FERC will adopt many of our suggested changes).  Judge [REDACTED] then basically describes both proposals of the [REDACTED] Group and the Southern, etal. group, then provides the stakeholder participants' comments (almost verbatim from all submissions to her).  While she states that the process should keep moving quickly, she does not adopt any timeframes (we will comment that FERC should retain its Dec 02 Day 2 start date (for LMP/financial rights congestion model to be implemented).
 
The attachments below are her report, the Southern, etal proposal and the [REDACTED] pricing, congestion management, operating, and planning protocols (without any changes).  Clearly, our response FERC will include our proposed changes to the documents.  [REDACTED] from her report below:

She recommends that SPP pursue an RTO coalition in the Midwest 
She encourages FERC to continue to encourage and utilize a collaborative process which accommodates stakeholders and state utility commission input, regardless of the RTO platform ultimately adopted by FERC 
Her opinion is that the "[REDACTED] (Entergy, [REDACTED]South and [REDACTED]Florida utility sponsored) Model" (transco in RTO, LMP/financial rights congestion management) is better developed and more clearly in compliance with the requirements of [REDACTED]. 2000 based on a "best practices" analysis of other RTOs and Commission precedent.  This is good because Enron generally favors the [REDACTED] model. However, she does provide the Commission information on Southern, etal.'s proposal (independent system administrator--like National [REDACTED]-type) in her report, even though she has concerns that the Southern, etal model is not [REDACTED]. 2000 compliant.  [REDACTED], etal. model has a hybrid physical congestion management with day ahead balanced schedule requirements and LMP in real time (with penalties) and significant control area responsibilities.  The significant aspects of the [REDACTED] model are discussed below: 
[REDACTED] Governance: RTO is a for profit transco with an independent board and an enhancement:  the [REDACTED] (IMA).  [REDACTED] was added to facilitate public power participation (worried that transmission owner on top, even if divested from generation, would favor its transmission solutions over generation or other transmission solution).  [REDACTED] would administer the markets; exercise operational authority over the transmission system; administer one OASIS (with one process for interconnection and transmission requests); and assume the [REDACTED] function.  She strongly encourages this split. 
Good stakeholder advisory Committee (provides majority and minority views) and an independent [REDACTED] to monitor the RTO and the markets 
Allows for [REDACTED] (Entergy wants to be one), with RTO oversight 
Describes that control area operators will initially maintain their control areas for physical operation, such as switching transmission elements, pursuant to operating procedures approved by the RTO/IMA.  Recognizes that consolidation of control areas may occur as RTO matures.  ([REDACTED] should be subject to further discussion) 
RTO/transco has primary responsibility for tariff administration, including rate changes and tariff filings 
All load under the rates, terms and conditions of OATT ([REDACTED]South group has appealed this part of earlier Commission orders, and, thus, retains their rights should they win on appeal) 
4 transmission pricing zones initially: Entergy, Southern, [REDACTED]South, and [REDACTED]Florida.  Allows for participant funded, direct assigned, and merchant transmission.  Transmission rates to loads in RTO will be zonal (for existing facilities) and regional charge (for new facilities, other than those above).  There will be one "through and out rate", plus a [REDACTED] [REDACTED] for RTO operation. 
Encourages conversion of existing transmission agreements to OATT 
[REDACTED]/LMP (includes "real time spot market") is clearly the preferred model and represents best practices model (from PJM and SPP) -- many details need to be worked out in continued discussions.  Model allows parties to transact bilaterally and provides financial congestion hedges (FCH).  Stakeholder process needs to be continued for FCH allocation, auction and non-discriminatory release.  The [REDACTED] model includes a "balancing resource" requirement that we will provide comments against 
Will allow establishment of markets for ancillary services once they can be supported -- control area operators required to provide before such market establishment 
RTO runs single OASIS and independently calculates ATC and TTC 
RTO/transco is responsible for planning through a participatory stakeholder process 
Numerous comments from marketers, IPPs on reduction of control area functions (all of our comments from last week's response are included) 
Judge [REDACTED] had numerous meetings with state commissioners.  (1) Many are still concerned that FERC had not conducted a "cost-benefit" analysis and had concerns over the costs.  She responds that extrapolation of costs to the entire [REDACTED] seems unwarranted because it ignores the "economies of scale" and initial reliance on existing control centers and infrastructure.  In any event, FERC could explore the feasibility of a rate setting process that would involve the state commissions. (2) concerned about cost shifting:  mediation team discussed phase in of rates that the Commission has allowed. (3) Concern that [REDACTED] would preempt state ability to approve asset transfers to RTO and want state retail customers to receive full value for assets.  (4) concern that [REDACTED] may prematurely put Southern states into retail open access (concerns with [REDACTED].)  Judge encourages the Commission to utilize a collaborative process that includes input from state commissioners.

I think our message for any press discussions tomorrow should focus on the extremely beneficial aspects of the mediation and the significant "jump start" it gave to the [REDACTED] process due to the significant role played by Judge [REDACTED] and her team on focusing the discussions.  Furthermore, the initial results after 45 days show the majority of participants favoring a "best practices" PJM/SPP cornerstone type real time spot energy market with LMP pricing and financial congestion hedges and an RTO with an independent board structure that accommodates a for-profit RTO entity with a significant stakeholder advisory process.  [REDACTED] should consider utilize the fundamentals resulting from this mediation for other regions, or, at a minimum, consider similar mediated "jump starts."

Team,

I’ve sketched the quick triage you asked for.  Happy to wordsmith once we agree on the guts.

NEEDS (non-negotiable)
1.  Any settlement must preserve our right to collect the $**M fuel-cost true-up we booked in Q2.  If we give that up we eat the charge and kill guidance.
2.  Language that expressly carves Enron out of any prospective “joint & several” penalty exposure.  We will NOT sign if we can be dragged back in for downstream disgorgement.
3.  A hard-wired sunset on the compliance reporting obligations—18 mos max, not the 5-year window the ISO is pushing.
4.  Confidentiality clause tight enough to keep our bidding

> > I believe that a more appropriate description of the role of the MMU in
> > evaluation of the RTOs is to describe the role as one of evaluating the
> > effectiveness of RTO rules, operations or behavior in promoting the
> > development of electricity markets.  In other words does the RTO ensure
> > fair
> > and open access to the transmission system?  Does it promote a robust and
> > transparent market for transmission? Does the operation of the RTO promote
> > robust and transparent energy markets by decreasing barriers to entry
> > (removing geographic and temporal constraints and arbitrage opportunities)
> > and barriers related to resource allocation transmission vs. generation
> > additions or demand side responses.  I guess what I just described is
> > something other then an 'audit' who's meaning is somewhat more legalistic,
> > fiduciary, or governance in nature.

> > use.
> > I believe that a more appropriate description of the role of the MMU in
> > evaluation of the RTOs is to describe the role as one of evaluating the
> > effectiveness of RTO rules, operations or behavior in promoting the
> > development of electricity markets.  In other words does the RTO ensure
> > fair
> > and open access to the transmission system?  Does it promote a robust and
> > transparent market for transmission? Does the operation of the RTO promote
> > robust and transparent energy markets by decreasing barriers to entry
> > (removing geographic and temporal constraints and arbitrage opportunities)
> > and barriers related to resource allocation transmission vs. generation
> > additions or demand side responses.  I guess what I just described is
> > something other then an 'audit' who's meaning is somewhat more legalistic,
> > fiduciary, or governance in nature.

…[REDACTED]@caiso.com>, “[REDACTED], [REDACTED]” <[REDACTED]@ferc.gov>

Subject: Re: SSG-WI work-plan – next steps

All,

Quick update from my end: I just locked in the 8:30 PST slot for Thursday.  
Bridge will open 10 min early; same pass-code as last week.  If anyone needs it re-sent, ping me off-thread.

On substance—after re-reading the “clean” version Kristi circulated, I still see two flash-points we have to settle internally before we put fingerprints on this:

1.  Scope of the MMU’s “evaluate” versus “audit” language.  
   Ottie’s point about avoiding the legalistic tone is well-taken, but the CAISO board counsel is already using “audit” in the draft charter that goes to the Board 12/6.  If we insist on “evaluate” we need alternate statutory language that still gives the unit subpoena power.  I can live with “evaluate and, where necessary, audit” if we define the latter as limited to rule-effectiveness, not corporate fiduciary duty.  Thoughts?

2.

From: [REDACTED] [mailto:abb@eslawfirm.com]
Sent: Thursday, October 25, 2001 10:36 AM
To: [REDACTED]; doug kerner; [REDACTED]
Subject: highlights from CPUC meeting
Importance: High


FYI.  [REDACTED] from today's CPUC meeting.  It is still in progress,
with few energy items remaining.  Regular results memo to follow.

CPUC opens new rulemaking to return to "integrated resource planning"
with contemplation of process that sounds much like the discredited
[REDACTED] (BRPU) proceeding.  Clearly contemplates
utility construction of new generation projects, but also calls for
life-cycle cost analysis of all options, including new asset
construction, power purchase agreements, conservation, transmission,
etc.

CPUC rejects new interstate transmission facilities as not needed for
reliability purposes (lines to NV, AZ and MX).  Will begin new phase re
economic projects.

CPUC rejects request for clarification regarding QF contract
amendments.  Parties had requested clarification from CPUC decision
based on language in [REDACTED] (ALJ) ruling that stated extensions
could go forward until the Commission acts.  CPUC states that parties
remain free to negotiation changes, but that they must move forward on
ordinary review process.  This decision primarily undermines QF contract
amendments with SCE's.

CPUC rejects PG&E's call for market valuation of retained generation.
PG&E argued that CPUC must use market value of retained assets to set
cost of service rates.  CPUC rejects argument.  Duque will concur, but
notes that there are outstanding issues including the likelihood that
CPUC will fail to meet statutory deadline to market value retained
assets, and fact that PG&E still has stranded cost recovery rights that
require market valuation.  It is also unclear, notwithstanding the CPUC
settlement with SCE, how post-transitions period costs will be recovered
per that agreement.


--
[REDACTED]
Sacramento, CA

From: [REDACTED]  
Sent: Thursday, October 25, 2001 4:17 PM  
To: [REDACTED], [REDACTED]; Steffes, [REDACTED]; [REDACTED], [REDACTED]; [REDACTED], [REDACTED]; Comnes, [REDACTED]; Kaufman, [REDACTED]  
Subject: RE: [REDACTED] -- [REDACTED] “[REDACTED]”  

All,

1.  PURPA angle  
   • The Commission’s silence on QF amendments is not accidental.  By forcing every change into the ordinary-review track they are effectively daring the QFs to file a §210(h) complaint at FERC.  Once that happens, the state’s “avoided-cost” benchmark gets litigated in D.C., not San Francisco.  If FERC finds the CPUC’s administratively-determined avoided cost is below the QF’s LCOE, the QFs can walk from the CDWR slice-and-dice deals and keep their 15-year PPAs

Subject: Re: CalPX refund-allocation language – does CAISO have a parallel clause?

[REDACTED] – 13 Aug 2001 08:42 a.m.

1.  Bottom line up front  
   a.  CalPX is telling FERC: “If you don’t tell us how to slice the pie, we’ll slice it ourselves using the share-fraction method.”  
   b.  CAISO’s tariff is silent on the mechanics of passing refunds *through* to loads; it only speaks to refunds *to* CAISO.  That gap gives CAISO the same practical option CalPX is reserving—if FERC doesn’t order a specific methodology, CAISO can adopt any “reasonable” allocator (and its default is pro-rata on a load-ratio-share basis).  

2.  What “share-fraction” means in CalPX-land  
   CalPX calculates each participant’s percentage of total **demand** (MWh cleared) during the refund period and applies

From: [REDACTED]@ENRON [mailto:[REDACTED]@ENRON.com]

Subject: RE: CalPX refund allocation – do we care?

Couple of quick points:

1. CalPX “share-fraction” method  
   - They take the MWh a buyer cleared during the refund period and divide it by the total MWh cleared by all buyers.  
   - Net effect: the biggest volume players get the biggest refund checks.  
   - We were ~8 % of CalPX demand in Q2 2000, so under their default rule we’d pick up roughly that same share of whatever pot FERC lets them distribute.  
   - Upside: could be eight-figure cash. Downside: the pot is only the CalPX portion of the total overcharge, and everyone will fight over whether interest is included.

2. CAISO tariff – does it have the same “

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Continuation  

CalPX’s “share-fraction” rule would divide any refund pool in the same proportion that each buyer’s scheduled load bore to total CalPX load during the refund period (i.e., a buyer that accounted for 2 % of the ISO-integrated CalPX demand would receive 2 % of the dollars).  The formula ignores each buyer’s actual bilateral positions, out-of-market procurement, or CAISO real-time awards, so a large marketer that merely scheduled its portfolio through the exchange could end up with a windfall while a utility that self-provided most of its load and only bought 5 % through CalPX could recover almost nothing.  If FERC lets the exchange default to that method, two things happen:

1.  The Commission will have blessed a precedent that a market operator, not the regulator, decides who is harmed and in what amount.  
2.  Any later attempt to claw back and re-allocate money would require a new § 206 filing and another round of litigation—i.e., years of delay and interest leakage.

CAISO Tariff Comparison  
CAISO’s tariff (Section 11.7.3 & 11.8.4, revised 3/22/01) already contains a safety valve: “

Subject: Re: Tomorrow’s Refund Call – Draft Agenda & Open Issues  

All,

Howard’s note below is the last thread we have before the call kicks off at 9 a.m. CDT tomorrow.  I’ve taken a first cut at an agenda (attached) and flagged the items that are still open.  The big-ticket questions are:

1. Refund Offset Mechanics  
   – Do we all agree that the ISO’s proposed “net-back” method (ISO Tariff § 36.5) is the only practical way to get money out the door by 9/30?  
   – If we adopt it, do we give up the right to pursue the alternate “gross-up” methodology later?  Cargill is still pushing the gross-up, but Coral and Avista say it will blow the timeline.

2. Interest on Late Payments  
   – FERC’s 8/17 order is silent on interest for the 2000-2001 vintage invoices.  Bracepatt’s memo says we can live with zero interest if we get the principal by year-end; Fulbright disagrees and wants to reserve the right to file a § 205 complaint later.  We need a consensus position before tomorrow or the ISO

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
[REDACTED]  
Sent: Monday, August 20, 2001 2:22 PM  
To: [REDACTED], [REFF]; Steffes, [REDACTED]; Mara, [REDACTED]  
Subject: RE: [REDACTED]'s [REDACTED]  

Yes—just pulled the term sheet.  If we embed a fixed-for-floating swap inside the usage contract we can label it a “price-protection rider.”  Customer elects it at signup, no separate ISDA, all handled through the utility tariff.  Swap notional floats with monthly kWh, resets at Houston Ship Channel index + 150 bp.  We lay the risk off on the ICE screen same day.  

Margin comes from two places:  
1.  30 bps embedded in the tariff rate (customer never sees the breakout).  
2.  1×1 matching book—whatever volume the customer takes, we source the physical gas and mirror the swap.  Locking in 8–12 bps on the round-turn after credit reserve.  

Downside is regulatory discovery: if the PUC decides this is a “financial product” they may force us to un

Subject: RE: ERO Governance Structure – Next Steps

All,

Following yesterday’s call, I’ve spoken with the General Counsel’s office.  They confirmed that the Commission can lawfully prescribe the bylaws of the Electric Reliability Organization (ERO) down to the committee level, including the right to remove any director—elected or appointed—by written order.  In short, FERC can retain “negative” control (approval / veto) or “positive” control (direct appointment), but we cannot allow the ERO to become a private club that writes its own rules and then asks us to bless them after the fact.

Action items:

1. Draft Order language will be circulated by COB Monday.  It will contain:  
   a. A non-delegation clause: the ERO may not assign any standard-setting or enforcement function to a regional or subordinate body without prior FERC approval.  
   b. A hard-wired 4-year sunset on every reliability standard, forcing a Commission review.  
   c. A removal provision: FERC may remove any board member for “cause” defined as (i) failure to follow Commission directive, (ii) self-dealing, or (iii) material violation of the Commission’s rules or

Continuation of the message body:

Enron’s Regulatory Affairs team has reviewed SDG&E’s Motion and the proposed Memorandum of Understanding.  While the MOU contains several provisions that could accelerate cost recovery for SDG&E, it also creates a precedent that may affect how stranded-plant costs are allocated across all California IOUs.  Of particular concern:

1. The MOU would allow SDG&E to securitize the remaining balance of its generation-related regulatory assets through a non-bypassable “exit fee” that applies to all customers—including those who migrate to Direct Access after January 1, 2002.  If the Commission adopts this mechanism for SDG&E, we expect Southern California Edison and Pacific Gas & Electric to file similar requests within weeks.  That could materially reduce the competitive footprint for ESPI (Enron Energy Services) in the C&I market.

2. The MOU is silent on how the securitized charges would be treated if a municipalization drive succeeds in SDG&E’s service territory.  A successful muni formation would shift the payment obligation to a shrinking pool of remaining customers, potentially triggering a “death spiral” that the Commission has historically resisted.

3. The MOU would grandfather SDG&E’s QF (

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
4.  The phrase “later phase” is a litigation-sinkhole.  Once the docket recites that something is merely deferred, every disappointed claimant will file a motion to “lift the stay,” “supplement the record,” or “reopen the proof.”  We will spend the next eighteen months briefing whether the judge still has jurisdiction rather than whether anyone owes money.  If we truly want this case to expire, the order should state explicitly that any claim whose validity depends on an initial finding of refund liability is DISMISSED WITHOUT PREJUDICE and may be pursued, if at all, only in a separate proceeding filed in the appropriate district court.  No certificate of final judgment under Rule 54(b) should issue until that dismissal is on the face of the order.

5.  The “ballpark estimate” language is worse than silence.  It invites every witness to hedge: “I can’t quantify today, but my rough sense is somewhere between X and ten-times-X.”  Once those numbers are in the transcript, every subsequent brief will treat the high end as the low end and demand discovery to ‘refine’ it.  We should ask the judge to strike any testimony that purports to reserve or estimate a ripple claim unless

4.  If we do not exclude ripple claims now, the judge will treat the order as a jurisdictional placeholder.  Every brief, every expert report, every discovery request will be written with one eye on “Phase II,” turning the current record into a procedural footnote.  The Commission staff will file a status report six months from now saying “the parties have identified 1,200 potential ripple claimants; we need a further order governing discovery and a revised schedule.”  The ALJ will sigh, shrug, and give them one.  Once that happens, the docket number on this case will outlive the careers of the associates who opened it.

5.  Worse, the very act of “deferring” invites collateral attack.  A customer who bought power from us at a price that embeds the disputed refund will argue that the Commission’s failure to rule on ripple claims is an arbitrary-and-capricious “final agency action.”  They will sue in the D.C. Circuit tomorrow, asking the court to compel the Commission to “complete” the proceeding.  The court will not care that we never wanted the proceeding in the first place; it will care only that the Commission left the door ajar.  We will then be defending

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),

could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
gmathews@edisonmission.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
…4.  The phrase “ballpark estimate” is itself a trap.  The judge may have said it in passing, but once it is memorialized in an order it becomes a benchmark.  If a party later submits an estimate that is higher (or lower) than the ballpark, opposing counsel will wave the transcript and accuse us of bad-faith sand-bagging.  The safer course is to strike every reference to any “estimate,” ball-park or otherwise, and replace it with a sentence that simply reserves the right to supplement the record “if and when the Commission determines that supplemental information is necessary.”

5.  The draft order’s silence on preclusion is louder than its words on deferral.  We all know how the next phase will unfold: the ALJ will issue a certification order that says “Parties had an opportunity to identify ripple claims in Phase 1 and elected not to do so; accordingly, any claim not identified is waived.”  We need language that explicitly preserves the right to assert ripple claims in any subsequent refund proceeding, investigation, or complaint—even if this docket is closed.  A single sentence—“Nothing in this order shall be construed to preclude any party from asserting any claim or defense in any other proceeding”—

meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against

term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
4.  A fourth, more tactical, concern: if the order “defers” ripple claims but does not extinguish them, we may inadvertently arm the judge with a vehicle for revisiting the refund issue the moment this docket quiets down.  Every brief we file from now on will have to carry a footnote reserving rights on ripple claims, which signals to the Commission (and to the press) that we are hedging our bets.  That undercuts our “no-refunds-ever” narrative.  Worse, if a later phase is created, the Commission staff—not us—will draft the discovery schedule.  Once staff controls the timetable, we lose the ability to starve the record of data and witnesses, which is our best insurance against a fact-intensive damages model we cannot control.

5.  The proposed silence on interest is equally dangerous.  The draft neither affirms nor denies that interest would run on any ripple award.  If we say nothing now, a future order can simply incorporate the default interest rate under 18 C.F.R. § 35.19a (the Commission’s late-payment rule) compounded from the transaction date.  That turns a molehill into a mountain.  We should insist on language that “any and

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
4.  The more I stare at the language the judge is prepared to sign, the more I worry we are walking into an estoppel trap.  Paragraph 3 says “parties need not quantify ripple claims at this time,” but the very next sentence invites “any party that believes it may possess ripple claims” to file a statement “preserving” them.  Once that statement is on the docket it will be Exhibit 1 in every subsequent brief arguing that we “waived” anything we do not list.  The safe play is to file a one-page placeholder that simply incorporates by reference every conceivable claim, counterclaim, cross-claim, set-off, recoupment, surcharge, or defense that might ever arise out of any transaction touching the western energy markets from 1997 forward.  Yes, it looks ridiculous, but it beats being told two years from now that we “forfeited” a nine-hundred-million-dollar refund because we failed to cite the exact invoice number in 2001.

5.  “Later phase” equals litigation purgatory.  The judge’s docket already runs to 2025 on the current schedule.  If we leave ripple claims “deferred,” every disappointed respondent will file a motion to

4.  A fourth concern, one that did not crystallize until I re-read the draft order in the quiet of last night: the judge’s “ball-park estimate” language is not anchored to any evidentiary standard.  If we remain silent now, we may later be confronted with an opponent who offers a back-of-the-envelope extrapolation that is dressed up as a “reasonable” estimate yet is incapable of audit or cross-examination.  I would add one sentence to the order: “Any ripple-calculation proffer must be accompanied by the data set, methodology, and underlying work-papers that would allow an opposing expert to replicate the result.”  That single sentence keeps us from being rolled by a number no one can unpack.

5.  The draft’s silence on preclusive effect is deafening.  We all assume that if we defer ripple claims we are not waiving them, but the order never says so.  A one-line reservation—“Nothing in this order shall be construed to preclude any party from asserting ripple claims in any subsequent proceeding”—costs us nothing and buys us peace of mind.  Without it we are gambling that a future judge will read our silence as strategic rather than as a waiver.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Three comments ("severe" concerns?) which I am sorry I was unable to
deliver before the draft order went out to all parties:

1.  I share [REDACTED] concern.  Although the judge did say at
Monday's prehearing conference that she would accept a party's "ballpark
estimate" of its ripple claims, the express inclusion of a procedure for
ripple claim offers of proof could later harm a party that remains silent
on this issue, despite the draft order's statement that offers of proof are
"not required."

2.  The draft order might benefit from an up-front definition of ripple
claims.  First, though, we need to make sure that we all agree on the
meaning of the terms that we are using.  Depending on how we define ripple
claims, it seems to me that a counterclaim or a cross-claim could indeed be
a ripple claim, and we need to be sure that we are not prejudiced by
failing to assert such claims now.  A counterclaim is my claim against
somebody who has asserted a claim against me.  A cross-claim is my claim
against a third party who has NOT asserted a claim against me.  (As for
"offset," I have been under the impression that we have been using that
term to describe a deduction that may validly be made from a refund amount
that I am deemed to owe another party -- particularly, a deduction
occasioned by that other party's unpaid bill from me.)  Presumably, each of
us knows who sold power to us and the prices for those sales.  Therefore,
should I so choose, I already have the ability to assert a refund claim
against any of the entities that sold to me -- whether the seller has made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a counterclaim) or has not made
a claim against me (making my refund claim a cross-claim).  But given my
opposition to ANY refunds in this case, I would prefer to refrain from
asserting my own refund claims (whether counterclaims or cross-claims),
while reserving my right to do so in the future should the need arise.
Perhaps we should define a ripple claim simply as any refund claim that
could potentially be asserted by a party that has expressed an opposition
to refunds in this case.  That way, we could maintain a unified front of
opposition to refunds without being prejudiced in any way from seeking our
own refunds if it should come to that.  In testimony and other filings, we
would make it clear that any refund directive would open the Pandora's box
of wave after wave of ripple claims, but we would not need to quantify them
now.

3.  The draft order, in the title, "defer[s] ripple claims to a later phase
in this proceeding."  We want this proceeding to die -- not to live on in
later phases.  Shouldn't the order simply EXCLUDE ripple claims as beyond
the scope of the proceeding?

4.  A fourth, more strategic worry: every day the order sits out there in “defer-not-exclude” form is a day our adversaries can mine the docket for sound-bites about how “even the judge expects a second shoe to drop.”  The press, already primed for headlines about “billions in hidden claims,” will treat the phrase “later phase” as judicial confirmation that the pot of money is real.  Once that narrative hardens, settlement leverage flips against us.  A simple clerical erratum now—strike “defer” and substitute “exclude as outside the scope of this proceeding”—costs us nothing and denies them that talking point.

5.  Finally, the confidentiality clause in § 12 of the draft is porous.  It allows parties to file “public versions” that omit only “proprietary numbers.”  A ripple claim, even if unquantified, is still a litigation position.  Once we file a public version saying “Respondent X reserves the right to seek refunds from any seller that benefitted from the rates under challenge,” the headline writes itself: “Enron affiliate signals $2 billion counter-assault.”  We should insist that any reservation of ripple rights be filed under seal, with the
